Workplace accidents include all accidents causing harm to human health or death and that are related to work at the workplace, while working off-site or during home office/remote work and all accidents on the direct way between home and workplace on the way to and from work.

Dangerous occurrences (“événements dangereux” / “gevaarlijke voorvallen”) are certain serious, legally defined near-miss events in the workplace that had the clear potential to cause serious harm (to health, life, or property) but did not result in an actual injury or accident.\n\nDangerous Occurrences (Annexes I.6-1 and I.6-2):

### Low Workhours

 Are your hours and/or employee count entered correctly? The total number of hours reported are considerably *LOWER* than the average weekly hours typically worked by full-time employees. 

Please validate that you have entered your correct employee count and/or total hours. If correct, provide a brief explanation for approval (this will be visible to your client connections). Otherwise, please correct your answers.

### High Workhours 

Are your hours and/or employee count entered correctly? The total number of hours reported are considerably *HIGHER* than the average weekly hours typically worked by full-time employees. 

Please validate that you have entered your correct employee count and/or total hours. If correct, provide a brief explanation for approval (this will be visible to your client connections). Otherwise, please correct your answers.

Serious occupational accidents are work-related accidents that, by law, require an in-depth investigation due to their severity, as defined in Article I.6.2 of the Code on Well-being at Work.\nExamples include structural collapses, failure of safety equipment, falls from significant heights, or incidents with life-threatening consequences.
Please enter the total number of serious occupational accidents for the given year.

Include all work-related accidents where professional medical treatment was provided (such as stitches, physical therapy, or prescription medication), but the injured employee did not miss any days of work.
Please enter the total number of occupational accidents that required medical treatment beyond first aid but did not result in time away from work for the given year.

Include minor work-related accidents that were treated on-site using basic first aid (e.g., cleaning and bandaging small cuts, applying ice packs) without requiring professional medical treatment or any days away from work.
Please enter the total number of occupational accidents that required on-site first aid only and did not result in time away from work for the given year.
